title: Domain Pipeline Design
use_for: designing domain-specific dataflow from sampled inputs to computed state and commands
separating telemetry, analysis, decisions, external mutation, and UI/debug publication
making domain state replayable or inspectable
do_not_use_for: external API reverse engineering by itself
rules: sample external inputs before domain stages run
keep analysis stages free of external mutation
publish stable read-only state for UI, diagnostics, and tests
route external changes through command or applier stages
make stage contracts explicit enough for replay and validation
procedure: Name the domain outcome the pipeline is responsible for.
Define input snapshots, configuration, persistent state, transient state, published state, and command outputs.
Split stages by responsibility: sample, normalize, analyze, decide, publish, apply.
Define stage order, invalidation rules, and ownership of each data structure.
Define diagnostics and replay artifacts for representative frames or events.
Validate that UI and external appliers consume published state or commands, not transient internals.

primary_when: the task is primarily about shaping domain dataflow and stage ownership
must_not_own_when: the task is only about runtime host lifecycle
the task is only about visual presentation

solves: how to turn live input data into deterministic domain decisions
how to keep analysis separate from mutation
how to expose domain state safely to UI and diagnostics

core_principles: A domain pipeline should explain how data becomes decisions.
Stage boundaries should protect ownership and validation, not just organize files.
Published state is a contract for observers; transient state is an implementation detail.
External mutation belongs at the edge after decisions are made.
constraints: Do not let analysis stages mutate external systems.
Do not let UI read transient working buffers.
Do not hide configuration reads inside unrelated stages.
Do not make stage order depend on incidental registration order.
decision_rules: If data is sampled from outside the domain, convert it into an input snapshot.
If a stage makes a decision, name its required inputs and outputs.
If external state must change, emit a command and apply it at a boundary.
If UI needs visibility, publish a stable view model or diagnostic snapshot.
If a bug is hard to reproduce, add replayable inputs or frame diagnostics.

preferred_patterns: diagnostic stage timing and state summaries
avoid_patterns: stage methods reaching into host APIs
UI and appliers sharing mutable domain internals
decision logic inside rendering or logging code

recipes.name: Design a mechanics pipeline
recipes.when: Use when a domain capability depends on sampled runtime data and produces decisions or mutations.
steps: Define the capability outcome in domain language.
Create input snapshots for all external data.
Split computation into analysis and decision stages.
Apply external mutations through a final boundary stage.

pitfalls: Calling any update method a pipeline.
Letting stages own both analysis and side effects.
Publishing live mutable state to observers.
Skipping replay artifacts for complex runtime behavior.
review_checklist: Is published state stable and read-only?
Are commands applied at an edge boundary?
Can representative behavior be inspected or replayed?

references.title: Stage Boundaries
references.interpretation: Use for splitting sample, analysis, decision, publish, and apply stages.
references.why: Domain pipelines depend on explicit stage ownership.

references.title: Dataflow State
references.interpretation: Use to separate input, transient, published, and command state.
references.why: Domain pipeline state should be inspectable and deterministic.
